Variable Definition: zCgCap represents total electric generating capacity from cogeneration by fuel source (which includes cogeneration and direct self generation). Cogeneration optionally can be simula+G12:G272ted at an aggregate sector level and/or it can be simulated at an individual industrial generating unit level. zCgCap is the total of electric generating capacity from sector level (zCgCapSector) and at the unit level (zCgCapUnit).zCgCap represents total electric generating capacity from cogeneration by fuel source (which includes cogeneration and direct self generation). Cogeneration optionally can be simulated at an aggregate sector level and/or it can be simulated at an individual industrial generating unit level. zCgCap is the total of electric generating capacity from sector level (zCgCapSector) and at the unit level (zCgCapUnit).

zCgCapSector represents electric generating capacity from cogeneration calculated at the sector level (opposed to unit level). zCgCapSector, which is by Fuel, is a transformation of the CgGCSector model variable, by Tech. The cogenerating capacity is a function of the steam generated or, in the case of direct generation, the capacity is a function of the electricity demand of each sector (CgDmdSector).

zCgCapUnit is the total of unit-level electric generating capacity (sum of unit capacity UnGC across all cogeneratng units). Initial unit generating capacities are input to the model through the future years as vUnGC through vData_ElectricUnits_CN.accdb.

zCgDemand represents the total electricity demand from cogeneration (by fuel source) and self generation modeled at the sector level (zCgDemandSector) and at the unit level (zCgDemandUnit). This includes both generation sold back to the grid and used as self generation, such as with rooftop solar PV. It is a summary variable by ECC and Fuel and is created y summing CgDmd across the ECs and Techs of the segments (R,C,I,T).

zCgDemandSector is defined as electricity demand for cogeneration modeled at the sector level only (opposed to the unit level). zCgDemandSector is output by fuel source and is a transformation of CgDmdSector which is by Tech (CgFrac is used to map Techs to Fuels).
zCgDemandUnit is defined as electricity demand for cogeneration modeled at the unit level. It is the sum of UnDmd across generating units that are flagged as cogenerators (vUnCogen=1).
zCgGen represents the total electricity generated from both sector-level cogeneration  (zCgGenSector) and unit level cogeneration (zCgGenUnit).

zCgGenSector is defined as electric generation from cogeneration modeled at the sector level only (opposed to unit level). It is output by fuel source and is a transformation (via CgFrac) of the model variable CgEGSector by Tech.
zCgGenUnit is defined as electric generation from cogeneration modeled at the unit level. It is the sum of electric generation by unit, UnEGA, for all cogenerating units (indicated by vUnCogen=1).

Descriptor: Conversion Market Share Fraction by Device
Variable Definition: The Conversion Market Share Fraction (zCMSF) represents the fuel/technology split that consumers will choose when replacing devices that have retired. The standard in the model is for consumers to replace retired devices with new devices of the same fuel/technology. However, conversions allow consumers to switch to a different fuel/technology when a device is retired. The CMSF is determined as a function of both price and non-price factors.

zDCC is defined as device capital cost of a typical device. It represents an aggregate of all makes and models of a specific technology (for example average capital cost for natural gas dryers). Once a level of device efficiency is chosen, the corresponding capital cost is known from trade-off curves contained in the model in the demand sector. A certain capital cost buys a certain level of efficiency.  It is used in the calculation of the marginal cost of fuel use (MCFU) in the demand sector.

Marginal Device Efficiency (zDEE) represents the efficiency of new devices and is calculated as the ratio of energy into the device to the useable energy out of it. The DEE is determined by relative fuel prices (either excluding or including pollution costs - DEEPrice or DEEPoll).  As prices rise, marginal device efficiencies rise as well. Once DEE is known, Device Capital Costs (DCC) are also known through the estimated trade-off curves. How much additional device efficiency will be selected as prices rise depends on consumer preferences for higher upfront capital costs over higher operating costs in the future. The national values reported in the output files are weighted by region based on production capacity (EUPC). The limits of the efficiency curve are constrained by device efficiency maximums (DEM) as well as by existing or new device efficiency standards (DESTD). Adjustments to the curve can be made by through a device efficiency maximum multiplier (DEMM).

zDEEA is defined as average device energy efficiency of all existing and new devices in stock. It is calculated from the marginal device efficiency (DEE) and process energy requirements (PER). In its historical initialization year, DEEA is set equal to the historical marginal efficiency (XDEE) the average then shifts over time as existing marginal efficiency levels and process energy requirements shift. If process energy requirements are low due to the dampening effect on the economy or high fuel prices, although device efficiencies at the margin will rise, there will be little impact on average device efficiency.

Descriptor: Marginal Market Share Fraction by Device
Variable Definition: The Marginal Market Share Fraction (zMMSF) represents the fuel/technology split that consumers will choose when purchasing new devices. The MMSF is determined as a function of both price and non-price factors. The price factors are the cost of using fuel (MCFU) and the marginal process efficiency (PEE). Non-price factors (MSMM) include the propensity to choose or not choose a particular device based on non-price fuel attributes, such as consumer perceptions. The calculation of MMSF is performed in the demand sector and it is used to split the production capacity additions (PCA) into Technologies and Enduses.
Retrofit Device Energy Efficiency (zRDEE) represents the marginal energy efficiency of retrofitted devices. The zRDEE currently is set equal to the input Retrofit Device Efficiency Standard (RDeStd).

Descriptor: Device Retrofit Market Share Fraction by Device
Variable Definition: Technology market share for retrofit devices as a ratio of 1.0. When retrofits are enabled in the model, the type of retrofit device is selected based on efficiency and cost parameters. All technologies for a sector are assumed to sum to 1.0 to reflected the entire market. Market shares can also be set exogenously depending on the value of the retrofit switch. Retrofits contain seperate market shares for process and device requirements to allow for detailed policy analysis

The zXDEEA is an exogenously-specified average device energy efficiency input to the model for a given technology (such as electric space heat). It represents the average efficiency across all existing and new devices in stock.

zDriver (equal to Driver) is the model variable that holds the economic driver of energy demand for each housing type, building type, industry, and transportation category. The units vary because the different sectors have different drivers of energy demand, such as households, floor space, gross output, etc.

Descriptor: Carbon Tax plus Permit Cost ($/eCO2 Tonnes)
Variable Definition: The maximum carbon prices is based on markets that cover CO2, where the highest value across all markets in a country sets this value. This is used as an input for the emission reduction cost curves.
Technology level fuel prices as paid by the consumer with all taxes and surcharges included.
Enduse energy demands divided by the econonic driver to produce an overall energy intensity. Produced only for output purposes.

ENPN is the price of energy in wholesale markets by fuel type in constant dollars per mmbtu. The current model version applies the same wholesale price to all areas. This price generally reflects the sale price of a fuel by producers for the relevant market. For example, the oil wholesale price will generally be the price of oil globally.
The exchange rate (Exchg) is an input to the model from TIM in $CN/$US.
zFloorspace represents building floor space and is the economic driver for residential and commercial buildings for the Canada areas of the model.

Delivered fuel prices are based on calibration from the input values (XFP), the wholesale fuel price (ENPN), and the fuel tax and delivery charge variables. FP is defined using the Prices set and can be used as an input to the consumer fuel price per technology (ECFP) after accounting for emissions costs. This usage of the variable could change or be depreciated as part of the CFS model code development.

FPBase holds fuel prices without fuel taxes (by technology) and is calculated based on the wholesale price (ENPN) and the calibrated delivery charge (FPDChg). This variable is produced for output purposes.

FPBaseF holds fuel prices without fuel taxes (by detailed fuel type) and is calculated based on the wholesale price (ENPN) and the calibrated delivery charge for each fuel type (FPDChgF). This variable is produced for output purposes.
Fuel prices by ECC and fuel type (FPECC) represents average delivered prices, excluding emission costs.
Fuel prices by ECC and fuel type (FPECCCFS) represents average delivered prices including the CFS prices.

Descriptor: Fuel Prices w/CFS and Carbon Price
Variable Definition: Fuel prices by ECC and fuel type (FPECCCFSCP) represents average delivered prices inclduing CFS and Carbon prices. It is equivalent to ECFP but translated into fuel type and ECC.

Descriptor: Fuel Prices w/CFS and Net Carbon Price
Variable Definition: Fuel prices by ECC and fuel type (FPECCCFSCPNet) represents average delivered prices inclduing CFS and Net Carbon prices.

Delivered price by detailed fuel type (FPF) is based on the wholesale fuel price, the fuel-level tax variables, and the fuel-level delivered charge that is calibrated from historical inputs. Fuel price variables by fuel are a newer model addition related to CFS code changes
FPPCost are fuel prices with emissions costs. It is equivalent to ECFP but translated into fuel type and ECC.
The portion of fuel price that is the sales tax on fuels by Prices set (Technology-Sector combinations) (FPSM) is an exogenous input with the ability to be adjusted by policies

The portion of fuel price that is the sales tax of fuels by detailed fuel type (FPSMF) is an exogenous input  and is applied to the delivered fuel price calculation (FpF). Sales tax by Fuel is an addition to the model as part of the Clean Fuel Standard modeling. Current values are based on the fuel sales tax by Prices set (or Technology-Sector) (FPSM).
The excise fuel tax (FPTax) is by the Prices set and is an exogenous input and can be individually adjusted by policies

The excise fuel tax (FPTaxF) is by the detailed Fuel type and is an exogenous input to the model. It is  applied to the delivered fuel price calculation (FpF). Excise tax by Fuel is an addition to the model as part of Clean Fuel Standard (CFS) modeling. Current values are based on the Price level tax input (FPTax)
The average floor space by building type (FSUnit) is input from the macro model and used with the number of households input to calculate total residential floor space.

The US GDP Deflator is read in from the macro model and is used as the input for Xinfla, which is the inflation index used by most of the model code to inflate or deflate dollars. A Canadian specific value is also read in for output purposes.
GDP by sector (GDPSector) is input from the macro model. It can optionally be used as an economic driver. It is currenty read into the model just for output purposes.
Gross output (GO) is input by sector (ECC) from the macro model. It is used as the driver for most industrial sectors.
Gross regional product (GRP) is input from the macro model and used as an economic driver for some sectors.
Number of households by sector (HHS) is input from the macro model. This value is used to calculate total households per area, which can be used as an economic driver
The inflation index (zInfla) is used to convert real to nominal values in the model.  It is calculated from the macro model output for US GDP Deflator.
Change in inflation (InflR) is read in from the macro model based on the US GDP deflator.
Labor force (zLabor) is read in directly from the macro model and can optionally be used as an economic driver for energy demands
Population by economic sector (Pop) is input from the macro model to be used as an input for market share equations
Total population (PopT) from the macro model is used as an economic driver for various sectors in the model
Real disposable income (RealDispInc) is read in directly from the macro model and can optionally be used as an economic driver for energy demands
Real Personal Income (RPI) data as input from residential sectors in the macro model. Can optionally be used as an economic driver
zGCPChained is an economic output directly reproduced from the macroeconomic model.

Historical generation by unit (XUnEGA) is used as an input for calibrating the electric generation model segment. It is primarily used to estimate a historical outage rate when compared with an input capacity value.
UnArea is used to identify which model Area a unit is located in. The value in UnArea is required to match a key in the Area set, soch as "ON" for Ontario.
Switch indicating if unit is industrial cogeneration. ‘0’ is used if the unit is solely for electric generation. ‘1’ is used if the unit is cogeneration.
Internal variable that counts the total number of units

Construction costs are calculated per unit using the contruction capital cost (UnGCCC) and the amount of capacity under construction (UnGCCR). This value is inflated for use in estimating unit net assets (UnNA) as part of the electric dispatch code.
Amount of fuel consumed per unit by fuel type per year. Produced as a function of unit generation, heat rate, and the fuel fraction

The availablity factor specifies the amount of total unit capacity that is available per model month. It is used as an input to develop a unit's effective generating capacity for use in electric dispatch
The efficiency of recharging storage for units. The total amount of energy stored is a function of total unit generation and the storage efficiency.

Total generation by month and time period (UnEG) is produced directly from the electric dispatch optimization routine to minimize system costs while meeting energy demands at each node. This value is aggregated into an annual figure (UnEGA) for use in the rest of the model.

Total annual electric generation (UnEGA) in gigawatt hours for each unit. This variable is a summation of the generation by month and time period (UnEG) determined by the electric dispatch optimization routine.
zUnEmit is a switch assigned to each unit indicating if unit produces emissions (‘0’ no emissions; ‘1’ unit emits).

UnF1 identifies the primary fuel type used by unit, where Natural Gas units typically have 'NaturalGas' listed as the UnF1. This value is used to select certain types of units for policies and model outputs.
zUnFacility assigns a facility name to units, grouping units into facilities. Facilities are typically used as part of emission reduction policies in the model.

The percentage of fuel type consumed over total fuel consumption for a given unit (UnFlFr), where the sum of all fuel types equals 1.0. A value for of 1.0 for a single fuel type means that the unit consumed only this single type of fuel. This is used with the unit heat rate and generation to produce the amount of fuel consumed

Generating capacity by unit (UnGC) is calculated based on exogenous inputs of capacity coupled with model generated capacity expansion as required based on reserve margins (or other user-specified methods to determine when to build new capacity).
Overnight capital costs for each unit (UnGCCC) in fixed dollars per kW of constructed capacity is an exogenous input to the model.

The amount of new capacity completed for each unit (UnGCCR) can be calculated endogenously in the capacity expansion routines or can be directly set annually by using the XUnGGCR variable. Endogenous capacity completed is an output of the capacity expansion module.
UnGenCo assigns the name of the generating company that owns each unit. For Canada units, the 'generating companies' are set equal to the name of the province/territory in which the unit resides.

The heat rate for each unit (UnHrt) is the annual ratio of Btu input per kilowatt hour output. The heat rate is used with the fuel fraction and unit generation to produce the amount of fuel consumed by the unit.
UnMust run is a user-specified switch to indicate the unit must always be dispatched if generation is needed regardless of market price. ‘1’ signifies that the unit is must run.
The name of the unit is read into the model for use in output purposes. Units can be selected using UnName as part of policies (which can be easier to read than the unit code).
UnNation is used to identify which model Nation a unit is located in. The value in UnNation is required to match a key in the Nation set, soch as "CN" for Canada.
Map to assign the unit to an electric node (UnNode). This is primarily used as input for electric dispatch to identify groups of units that generate to meet node-level loads
First year of operation for each electric unit (UnOnline).

The outage rate for each unit (UnOR) indicates the portion of a year in which a unit is unavailable to generate. This is typically used to represent any required regular downtime by the unit. A unit bids its effective capacity into the market, which is overall capacity (UnGC) adjusted for the amout of time it is offline. A value of UnOR=1.0 reflects that the unit was offline the entire year and 0.5 reflects that the unit was available roughly half the year.

UnOUREG is a units rate of own use generation. A portion of generation for each unit can be reserved for own use purposes, such as plant operation. This value specifies the percentage of overall unit generation reserved for own use

Descriptor: Own Use Rate for Generating Capacity
Variable Definition: UnOURGC is the amount of capacity used for own use.  A portion of capacity for each unit can be reserved for own use purposes, such as plant operation. This value specifies the percentage of overall unit capacity reserved for own use
Map that identifies the owner of a unit (UnOwner). Owners can be used as part of emission reduction policies in the model.
Map that selects the plant type of the unit (UnPlant). This map is used extensively in the model code and outputs to assign common plant characteristics
Average amount of emissions per fuel consumed per unit per year (UnPOCA) as a function of the unit's marginal emissions coefficient and the impact of emissions reduction devices
Total annual tonnes or kilograms of emissions by fuel type per unit by pollutant (UnPol).

UnRCGA indicates the amount of a unit's capital cost for equipment allocated to emission reductions. The UnRCGA is calculated as a function of the amount of reduction equipment added and the equipment cost. Total emission reduction capital cost is used as an input to produce overall unit net assets and is a portion of the unit fixed cost.

Annual reduction operation and maintenance costs per unit (UnRCOM). Reduction equipment constructed by a unit are assumed to have an annual operation cost, which is added to the overall unit fixed cost.
Last year of operation for each electric unit (UnRetire). Units that do not have a planned retirement date are set to an arbitrary year (2200) past the final year of model execution

UnSector maps the units to a specific industry (ECC). Cogeneration units can be assigned to specific demand sectors in the model for use in estimating sector electric generation. Non-cogen units are typically listed as 'UtilityGen'.
UnSource is a switch that identifies units that are endogenous.  It is set during model execution when a new unit is built.  The units listed the database are all assigned a value of 0.
Percentage of sequestered emissions of total emissions per unit (UnSqFr). Emissions released by the unit are the overall emissions produced minus the amount of emissions that are sequestered

UnStorage indicates whether a unit that is capable of storage so that effective capacity can be dispatched and storage can be recharged. An example of a storage unit is a hydro plant that can reserve water for peak load periods and refill water during minimal load periods.
Fixed operation and maintenance costs for each unit (UnFOMC). This is a portion of overall unit fixed costs and is an input for the bid price of electric units as part of the electric dispatch.

Operation and maintenance costs per amount of electric generated per unit (UnUOMC). This is a portion of the overall variable costs for each unit and serves as an input for the bid price of electric units as part of the electric dispatch.
UnXSw indicates to use exogenous data rather than model-calculated for specific units, such as oil sands cogeneration units.

UUnEGA holds the calibrated generation by unit. UUnEGA historically is the endogneously-calculated generation resulting from the model's dispatch optimization routine. In the forecast, UUnEGA is equal to the output variabel, UnEGA. For output purposes, in the historical period, unit generation (UnEGA) is typically set to equal the exogenously input historical data (XUnEGA). The calibrated historical generation by unit, UUnEGA, is used to identify issues with historical data (places where demand and supply historical data are unbalanced).
Exogenous electric generation (XUnEGA) is used as an input to calibrate the electric sector historically

User specified amount of generation capacity initiated (XUnGCCI) for a unit per year. Exogenous capacity initiated is added to any endogenous capacity initiated by the model to produce a total initiated capacity. Initiated capacity is added to the unit capacity annually based on the capacity completion rate
The cogeneration average pollution coefficient, CgPOCA, is calculated in the demand sector from embodied pollution divided by the tBtuS of cogeneration generation.

FsPOCX holds the emission coefficients for feedstock fuels (non-combusted fuels used as raw material input for processes). For GHGs, this value is an input to the model; for CACs, FsPOCX is calculated based on input inventories.

MEPOCX is the non-energy emissions coefficient - non-energy emissions released per unit of economic driver.  This value can read in directly or calculated by the model based on the input inventories and historical driver values.

Average amount of emissions per fuel consumed (POCA) reflects the emissions coefficient for the entire stock of existing devices in the model. This value can be calculated using different methods, such as via embedded emissions, by altering the value of the pollution switch (PolSw) for each pollutant.
Gratis emissions permits granted by a policy
Amount of emission permits needed to meet policy requirements (generally covered emissions minus gratis)
Emissions covered by an emission policy
Emissions cap specified by an emission policy

zCgDemand represents the total electricity demand from cogeneration (by fuel source) and self generation modeled at the sector level (zCgDemandSector) and at the unit level (zCgDemandUnit). This includes both generation sold back to the grid and used as self generation, such as with rooftop solar PV. It is a summary variable by ECC and Fuel and is created y summing CgDmd across the ECs and Techs of the segments (R,C,I,T).

Cogeneration Energy Demand (zCgDmd) is model-calculated projections of cogeneration. The Cogeneration Generation (CgEC) when multiplied by the Cogeneration Heat Rate (CgHrt) and is added to final net electric demand.

zDmd is a model output of end use energy demands by fuel type.  It is determined by Device Energy Requirements (DER) and modified by utilization factors such as eocnomic factors (CUF), non-price factors (CERSM), and weather.
DmdFPA represents energy demand from the electric utility sector by plant type and fuel.  These are the energy demands required by the electric supply sector to meet system-wide electricity demands.

Net energy demand (zNetDmd) represents total energy demand net of any sales by the sectors into the grid.  In the model, this is the variable TotDemand (which already has electricity sales netted out); however, some sectors sell steam to other sectors. So NetDmd is TotDemand minus any Steam sales resulting in steam values reported only for what each sector actually uses. The NetDmd variable additionally adds to TotDemand heat extracted from the air (heat pumps) or from the ground (geothermal). The model tracks the electricity used to pump this heat from the air or the ground (in TotDemand). This electricity usage is converted into the energy from the air and ground using the efficiencies, and put into NetDmd just for reporting purposes (not used by the model).

EuDemand represents enduse energy demands by fuel and ECC. It is an aggregation of the Dmd's within each of the residential, commercial, industrial, and transportation sectors (which are by EC and Tech) plus any enduse demands of the miscellaneous sectors/ECC's.
Output demands for non-combustion raw input fuels is calculated using the calibrated feedstock process efficiency with forecasted productivity capacity and the capacity utilization factor.
Output fuel demands for steam generation (StDmd). Calculated as a function of demand for steam and steam production heat rates
TotDemand represents the sum of the model's energy demand variables, including enduse demands, cogeneration demands, and feedstock demands by fuel type and sector (ECC).
Transportation energy demands (TrDmd) are calculated in the model based on projected energy requirements of each transportation mode.

Descriptor: Purchases from Areas in the same Country
Variable Definition: zAreaPurchases represents model projections of total electricity transferred/imported into a given area from other areas within the same country (province to province or state to state). The amount of imports are determined based on an LP optimizing system costs while meeting electricity demanded subject to capacity and transmission constraints.

Descriptor: Sales to Areas in the same Country
Variable Definition: zAreaSales represents model projections of total electricity transferred/exported out of a given area to other areas within the same country (province to province or state to state). The amount of electricity exports are determined based on an LP optimizing system costs while meeting electricity demanded subject to capacity and transmission constraints.

Coal production is an exogenous input to the model but is adjusted based on changes in prices which impact coal demand and coal exports.  Each area has a coal demand associated with it and is assigned a level of coal supply in by way of a coal supply switch,  CSSw (0=No Production; 1=Limited Production; 2=Unlimied Production). Some areas are set to exogenous coal production. In those cases, coal demand is met by coal production and imports as needed. Any excess coal goes to exports. Areas can also be a coal producing region with limited coal. Those regions produce no more than demand plus rest-of-world exports. A third category of regions has unlimited coal produciton. For those regions, coal exports are determined based on price. The coal production then is the coal demand of the region plus the exports.
zCExports are the model output of coal exports by province. These exports begin with exogenously-input levels adjusted based on changes in price.

zCgCap represents total electric generating capacity from cogeneration by fuel source (which includes cogeneration and direct self generation). Cogeneration optionally can be simula+G12:G272ted at an aggregate sector level and/or it can be simulated at an individual industrial generating unit level. zCgCap is the total of electric generating capacity from sector level (zCgCapSector) and at the unit level (zCgCapUnit).zCgCap represents total electric generating capacity from cogeneration by fuel source (which includes cogeneration and direct self generation). Cogeneration optionally can be simulated at an aggregate sector level and/or it can be simulated at an individual industrial generating unit level. zCgCap is the total of electric generating capacity from sector level (zCgCapSector) and at the unit level (zCgCapUnit).
zCgGen represents the total electricity generated from both sector-level cogeneration  (zCgGenSector) and unit level cogeneration (zCgGenUnit).
zCImports are the model output of coal imports by province. The imports start with exogenously-input levels and are adjusted based on changes in coal demands due to changes in price.

Coal adjustments (zCoalAdjustments) are used to modify the exogenous coal imports and exports in order to balance the demand-supply equation (coal demand plus coal exports minus coal production and coal imports).

zEGFA represents the total amount of electricity generated by fuel source across all individual generating units. Electric generation is determined using an optimization routine to meet energy demands and minimize system costs.
EGFAuu represents the model-calculated historical electric generation by fuel type. The LP optimization is run through history, and EGFAuu is the resulting optimal generation determined by the model.

EGPA represents the total amount of electricity generated by plant type across all individual generating units. Total electric generation by plant type is determined using an optimization routine to meet energy demands and minimize system costs.

EGPAuu is a calibration variable and represents the model-calculated historical electric generation by plant type. The LP optimization is run through history, and EGPAuu is the resulting optimal generation determined by the model.
Total exports (zExports) by fuel type and nation are output from the model and are calculated as the difference in the demand-supply balance (national supply minus demand plus imports).

Descriptor: Purchases from Areas in a different Country
Variable Definition: zExpPurchases are electricity purchases from areas in a different country (imports). The electricity purchases are determined from the electric dispatch model based on an LP meeting electricity demands while minimizing system costs.

Descriptor: Sales to Areas in a different Country
Variable Definition: zExpSales are electricity sales to areas in a different country (exports). The electricity sales are determined from the electric dispatch model based on an LP meeting electricity demands while minimizing system costs.

zFlows represents the annual electricity flows across the transmission lines. These annual flows are calculated by multipling the load by time period, HDLLoad, which is calculated in the dispatch routine, by the number of hours in each time period (HDHours)

Natural gas production by area (GAProd) for each type of gas production process is an output fo the oil and gas production moduel. The method of estimating gas production can vary or be set exogenous dependent on the gas production switches.
Electric generating capacity by fuel type (GCFA) is an aggregate of the individual units' generating capacities by fuel type.
Electric sector generating capacity by plant type (GCPA) is calculated as the sum of individual units' generating capacity by plant type.
Levelized cost of hydrogen production by production technology (H2Tech) and Area
Total hydrogen production by production technology (H2Tech) and Area

HDXLoad is exogenously-specified transmission line loads. Transmission line load can optionally be exogenous set for specific combinations of nodes, load time periods, and model months. This fixes line load to a single point and overrides any endogenous values.
Total imports (zImports) by fuel type and nation are output from the model and are calculated as the difference in the demand-supply balance (national supply minus demand minus exports).
Output variable that calculates the total annual losses per line by multiplying the total loads (HDLLoads) by the line efficiency (LLEff)

The maximum line load between nodes (LLMax) for each month and time period is an exogenous input set current set via text file. A value read in here between two nodes allows the electric dispatch to dispatch up to the specific limit for each period. The default value is zero (no load).
The amount of liquid natural gas production by area (LNGAProd) is currently an exogenous input to the model.

Oil production by area (OAProd) for each type of oil production process as estimated by the oil production module. The method of estimating oil production can vary or be set exogenous dependent on the oil production switches.

When there are emissions charges, such as a GHG tax, an emissions charge is calculated for new plants.  The value of offsets assigned to new plants (OffValue) is a function of the emissions permit price (ETAPr) and the amount of offsets assigned to new plants
Total emissions cost for new plants is based on the cost of emissions permits, emissions intensity, the level of gratis permits, and the type of fuel used by the unit minus any emissions offsets.
New plant emissions cost (PoTxRNew) are the cost of permits (ETAPr) times the emissions intensity (POCXNew) less the level of gratis permits (GPNew).
Power sold back to electric grid (PoSECC) is calculated based on the amount of cogeneration and the gross amount of electricity consumed.

RPPAdjustments is a calibration variable used to align historical data for fuel production in the model to account for issues with different data sources. The adjustment is equal to RPP demands plus input exports minus input RPP production and imports. The last value from the historical period is held constant for the model forecast.
Oil refinery RPP produciton by area (RPPAProd) is based on demand for refined petroleum products, imports, and exports depending on the value of the RPP switch.
Projections of refined petroleum product exports (RPPExports) are calculated based on RPP demand, imports, and production. Exports calculation can vary depending on the value of the RPP switch.
Projections of refined petroleum product imports (RPPImports) are calculated based on demands for RPPs. Imports calculation can vary depending on the value of RPP switch.

Electricity sales by sector (SaEC) represents electricity purchases from the grid. It excludes cogeneration. SaEC equals ESales - electricity from cogeneration + power sold to the grid (SaEC=ElecDmd-CgEC+PSoECC).

Calibration variable used to align historical data for fuel production in the model to account for issues with different data sources. The adjustment is equal to input demands plus exports minus production and imports. The last value from the historical period is held constant for the model forecast.

Transmission and distribution efficiency (TDEF) is directly set as an input to the model as a ratio, where 1.0 reflects no losses from transmission. This variable is used as an input to the electric load shapes to simulate that more electricity needs to be generated and transmitted than is actually demanded
TDEFLoss is an output variable that calculates the total annual electric sales losses per area as a function of electric sales (SAEC) and the distribution efficiency (TDEF)
Cogeneration investments (zCgInv) are determined by applying a cost of cogeneration capital (CgCC) to cogeneration rate of construction (CgCR).

Device investments (DInvTech) represent the amount of investment in end use technologies calculated as the capital cost, including taxes, retrofit capital costs, or exogenously input costs. This variables outputs by fuel type
Output variable for device investments (DInvTech) that converts investments to an annualized value using a 10% discount rate and the average device lifespan (DPL)
FuelExpenditures by ECC and Area is the total amount spent on fuel as calculated by multplying fuel demands by fuel price.
Total annual investments into new venting fugitive devices (FuInv). Investments are based on the amount of new reduction capacity constructed and the fugitive device capital cost.

Annual operation and maintenance costs for fugitive reduction devices (FuOMExp) calculated as a function of the capital cost of existing fugitive reduction devices and the fugitive operating cost fraction.
GCCCC is a user specified input variable by plant type equal to the unit direct capital cost in current dollars per kW.  It is used in the calculation of construction work in progress.

Descriptor: Cost of Energy from New Capacity
Variable Definition: Marginal Cost of Energy (MCE) is the total average cost per MWh for each plant type calculated by combining fixed and variable costs after accounting for the design hours of operation and any subsidies. This is a key input for the endogenous capacity expansion module, where plants types can be constructed based on the lower average cost.
Marginal Fixed Costs (MFE) are the fixed cost for new plants is a function of the construction cost, the capital charge rate and fixed operations costs
Marginal Variable Costs (MVC) are the variable costs for new plants include generation operation and maintenance costs, the fuel price, the plant heat rate, and the amount and cost of emissions.

OMExp are operating and maintenance expenditures and are calculated based on the device capital cost and an input ratio reflecting average annual maintenance cost per capital. For electric sectors, this value is based on the sum of operation and maintence costs for individual units (UnUOMC).

The Overage variable indicates pollution within an emissions market that are over the policy target. Markets that have overages can react in different ways depending on the market switches set by the policy.

Total expenditures on emissions permits (PExp) are a function of the permit price and the amount of permits bought to meet the policy compared to any permits that are sold as part of a cap and trade system. This can also include exogenously specified expenditures set directly by a policy.

Process investments (PInv) are calculated annually for demand sectors as a function of the process capital costs and amount of new capacity added after accounting for any process retrofiting. This value reflects the amount of spending on relevant structures for economic sectors, such as the cost of housing for residential sectors or the cost of factories for manufactuing sectors. The variable is output by fuel type.
Output variable for process investments (PInv) that converts investments to an annualized value using a 10% discount rate and the average process lifespan (PEPL)

Process investments by tech (PInvTech) are calculated annually for demand sectors as a function of the process capital costs and amount of new capacity added after accounting for any process retrofiting. This value reflects the amount of spending on relevant structures for economic sectors, such as the cost of housing for residential sectors or the cost of factories for manufactuing sectors. The variable is output by technology type.
Total annual investments into new sequestration devices (SqInv). Investments are based on the amount of new sequestration  capacity constructed and the sequestration device capital cost.

Annual operation and maintenance costs for sequestration devices (SqOMExp) calculated as a function of the capital cost of existing sequestration devices and the sequestration operating cost fraction.
Total spending on fuel taxes (TaxExp) are calculated as a function of total energy demands and the tax rate per fuel.
Unit Fuel Cost (UFC) is an output variable to show electric generation fuel costs by plant and power type. Calculated based on fuel price, heat rate, and node-level generation.

Unit Fixed operation and maintenance costs (UFOMC) are exogenously specified and are mulitiplied by the generation capacity to calculate the fixed cost component of total O&M costs. Used as an input for individual unit operation and maintenance costs.
Annual construction costs per unit (UnCW) are calculated as a function of the amount and cost of capacity current under construction.
Unit O&M costs (UOMC) are exogenously specified and are mulitiplied by total generation to calculate the variable cost component of  total O&M costs.
Total annual investments into new venting reduction devices (VnInv). Investments are based on the amount of new reduction capacity constructed and the venting device capital cost.

Annual operation and maintenance costs for venting reduction devices (VnOMExp) calculated as a function of the capital cost of existing venting reduction devices and the venting operating cost fraction.

zCgFPol represents the amount and type of pollution generated by fuels combusted during cogeneration activities. It is calculated by applying an emissions coefficient to the amount of fuel demanded for cogeneration.

zEndusePol is the total energy-related pollution by enduse and fuel type and EC which is also the model variable, Polute. It is calculated as the energy demand multiplied by the appropriate average pollution coefficient (POCA).

zEnFPol is the total energy-related pollution by fuel type and ECC equal to the model variable Polute, summed across enduse. Polut is is calculated as the energy demand (EuDem) multiplied by the appropriate average pollution coefficient (POCA).  EnFPol=Polute=EuDem*POCA
Output variable that produces energy emissions (EnFPol) with cogeneration emissions subtracted out.
Electric utility pollution (EUPol) is total pollution from the electric utility sector by fuel source, plant type, pollutant, and area.

Emissions due to gas flaring (zFlPol) from leaking gas into the air. Flaring is conducted in oil refineries, oil wells, and chemical plants and is often done as a safety measure to release over-pressuring of pipes. Emissions from flaring are calculated from a flaring emissions coefficient applied to the process emissions driver minus and flaring reductions obtained and plus any venting emissions from flaring CH4.

Total fugitive emissions by sector and pollutant (FuPol) represent emissions from leaks of gases into the air from fugitives other than venting or flaring. Calculated based on the fugitive emissions coefficient and the economic driver
Non-energy pollution (MEPol) is calculated as the product of the economic driver times a non-energy pollution coefficient for each tracked pollutant.

Non-combustion fuel emissions (NCFPol) are feedstock emissions (FsPol) aggregated across all ECCs. Feedstock emissions are calculated as demand for feedstocks times feedstock emissions coefficient. NCFPol=FsPol=FsDem*FsPOCA

SqPol represents the amount of emissions sequestered rather than emitted.  SqPol is negative and decreases the total emissions.  For electric units, sequestered emissions (EuPolSq) is determined by a unit sequestration fraction. For demand sectors, sequestered emissions are determined by constructed sequestration capacity (SqCap).  SqPol=0-SqCap; and for UtilityGen sector, SqPol=0-EUPolSq.

TotPol is total pollution for all types of pollution. It is the sum of energy-related, non-combustion, non-energy emissions, venting, flaring, other fugitives, off road non-energy pollution, and sequestering emissions (TotPol=EnPol+NCPol+MEPol+VnPol+FlPol+FuPol+ORMEPol+SqPol).
VnPol are total emissions from venting gases into the air. Calculated based on the venting emissions coefficient and the economic driver

Process Capital Costs (PCC) are capital costs for new structures in terms of annual amount of energy requirements added.  Capital costs are initialized using input data that estimate building construction costs for each sector. Capital costs changes are estimated for demand sectors based on process efficiency (PEE) and capital cost curve parameters. The results value is used as an input for the investment output

The marginal process efficiency across a particular enduse, reflecting the amount of process requirements needed per new unit of productive capacity. One example is the average amount of hot water required annually for a newly built house. It is calculated as a function of fuel cost (MCFU), the calibrated process efficiency multiplier (PEMM), and other parameters.

The Average Process Efficiency (PEEA) is the average process efficiency across a particular enduse, reflecting the amount of process requirements needed per existing unit of productive capacity. One example is the average amount of hot water required annually for all existing houses.  It is altered by changes in the Marginal Process Efficiency (PEE).  If the PEE is greater than PEEA, then PEEA should be increasing; the converse is also true.

PEMM, technological improvements in processes, is a calibrated variable reflecting non-price induced technological improvements which may reduce or improve process efficiency.  Processes that become more energy intensive, often by substituting capital for labor, cause more energy to be used per dollar of output. PEMM can change over time and these changes should be evaluated and connected to "real world" events.  Structural changes can show up in this variable as well.  PEMM is used as an input to calculate process efficiency (PEE) in the demand sector.
Process Energy  Requirements (PER) are calculated based on the value from the prior year, which is adjusted using overall Process Energy Requirement Additions (PERA) and overall retirements (PERR)

The final amount of process energy requirement added each year is a function of new production capacity (PERAPC), changes in saturation (PERADST), replacement of retired process requirements (PERAP), and additions from device conversions (PERARC)

The final amount of process energy requirement retired each year is a a function of retirements of production capacity (PERRPC), changes in saturation (PERRDST), retirement of processes (PERRP), retirements from retrofiting (PERRRR) and retirements from device conversions (PERRRC)

The determination of the level of retrofit process capital cost selected by consumers depends on the level of efficiency chosen which in turn depends on the price of energy.  ENERGY 2100 also captures technological improvements that can yield more efficiency for the same capital cost.  The value can be initialized based on the overall process capital cost (PCC) values or set exogenously using the retrofits switch.

Retrofit process efficiency reflects the amount of process energy required for retrofitting  and is calculated as a function of the marginal cost of fuel use and the retrofit parameters.  This variable is used to adjust the overall process energy stocks after accounting for retrofiting

Descriptor: Process Retrofit Market Share Fraction by Device
Variable Definition: Technology market share for retrofit devices as a ratio of 1.0. When retrofits are enabled in the model, the type of retrofit device is selected based on efficiency and cost parameters. All technologies for a sector are assumed to sum to 1.0 to reflected the entire market. Market shares can also be set exogenously depending on the value of the retrofit switch. Retrofits contain seperate market shares for process and device requirements to allow for detailed policy analysis

The Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF) differs from the Economic Capacity Utilization Factor(ECUF).  While ECUF picks up shifts in gross output, CUF is a calibrated variable reflecting energy demand fluctuations not explained by the other utilization factors.  Fluctuations in this variable should be random(no pattern of steady increase or decrease) when they occur and the variable's usual value is one.  The economic capacity utilization factor (CUF) depends on use or occupancy(determined by the economy) and is calibrated from the difference between historical sales and historical potential demand.  It modifies energy demand (DMD).
Output file that produces all capacity utilization factors (CUF) with no restrictions. The normal zCUF output is filtered to avoid producing too many irrelavant values

Production capacity by enduse (zEUPC) is the stock of production capacity obtained from the economic driver. EUPC is is modified by additions (EUPCA) and retirements (EUPCR) on a yearly basis. This variable forms the basis for determining the process energy requirements (PER) in the model.

Descriptor: Production Capacity Additions from New Production Capacity
Variable Definition: Production capacity additions (EUPCA) is a function of investments in production capacity (PCA) and marginal fuel shares (MSF).  The total additions to production capacity are split into fuel shares using the MSF.  It is calculated in the demand sector and is used in the determination of the process efficiency additions (PERA).

Descriptor: Production Capacity Retirements from Capacity Retirements
Variable Definition: EUPCRPC tracks capital stock from retirements due to failure and wear-out. Production capacity retirements depend on Production Capacity Lifetime (PCPL) and are withdrawn from the third capacity level. It is calculated in the demand sector and is used in the determination of the process efficiency retirements (PERRP).

FsPOCX_Trans are transportation feedstock emissions coefficient - for non-combusted fuels used as raw material input for processes. Value is read in directly for GHG pollutants and is calculated by the model based on input inventories for CAC pollutants. Transportation is produced seperately to account for more detailed technologies.

POCA_Trans is the average amount of emissions per fuel consumed reflects the emissions coefficient for the entire stock of existing devices in the model. This value can be calculated using different methods, such as via embedded emissions, by altering the value of the pollution switch (PolSw) for each pollutant. Transportation values are produced seperately to account for more detailed technologies.

TrMEPol holds total non-energy emissions for the transportation segment. Output inventories are based on the non-energy emissions coefficient, the level of economic driver, and the level of non-energy emissions reductions.
Emissions from fuel feedstocks in the transportation segment based on feedstock demands and the emissions coefficient.
Non-energy emissions for off-road vehicles in the transportation segment based on economic drivers and the emissions coefficient
Energy based emissions for the transportation segment. Output emissions based on input emissions coefficients and calculated energy demands.
Vehicle capital cost per process energy requirement addition per year. Produced by the model from cost curve parameters and used as an input for overall device investments output.

Marginal Efficiency of Vehicles (zTrDEE) represents the efficiency of new devices and is calculated as the ratio of energy into the device to the useable energy out of it. The DEE is determined by relative fuel prices (either excluding or including pollution costs - DEEPrice or DEEPoll).  As prices rise, marginal device efficiencies rise as well. Once DEE is known, Device Capital Costs (DCC) are also known through the estimated trade-off curves. How much additional device efficiency will be selected as prices rise depends on consumer preferences for higher upfront capital costs over higher operating costs in the future. The national values reported in the output files are weighted by region based on production capacity (EUPC). The limits of the efficiency curve are constrained by device efficiency maximums (DEM) as well as by existing or new device efficiency standards (DESTD). Adjustments to the curve can be made by through a device efficiency maximum multiplier (DEMM).

zDEEA is defined as average device energy efficiency of all existing and new devices in stock. It is calculated from the marginal device efficiency (DEE) and process energy requirements (PER). In its historical initialization year, DEEA is set equal to the historical marginal efficiency (XDEE) the average then shifts over time as existing marginal efficiency levels and process energy requirements shift. If process energy requirements are low due to the dampening effect on the economy or high fuel prices, although device efficiencies at the margin will rise, there will be little impact on average device efficiency.

TrDmd variable represents total transportation energy demand by transportation technology type. The name TrDmd is only used in the output files - the model variable is Dmd which is calculated based on vehicle energy requirements by model.

Transportation feedstock energy demand (TrFsDmd) represent non-combustion raw input fuel usage. TrFsDmd is the name used only in the output file; the model variable is named FsDmd and is calculated using the calibrated feedstock process efficiency with forecasted productivity capacity and the capacity utilization factor.

The Marginal Technology Market Share Fraction for the Transportation Sector (TrMMSF) represents the market share across technology types for new vehicles. A value of 1.0 for a technology reflects that all new vehicles required for the sector selected that technology.
Output process efficiency for transporation. Reflects the overall efficiency of the transportation system per sector in terms of miles required per level of economic driver
Vehicle or ton-miles traveled per year per transportation technology and sector as a function of energy demand and vehicle or tonnage efficiency. Ton miles are used in freight sectors.
Vehicle or ton-miles traveled per year per transportation technology and sector as a function of energy demand and vehicle or tonnage efficiency. Ton miles are used in freight sectors.

Vehicle Distance Traveled (zVDT, VDT) is a measure of the level usage of the various types of vehicle technologies within a given area. It is an output variable in the transportation sector calculated as the amount of fuel demand times the average vehicle efficiency (VDT=Dmd*DEEA).

Descriptor: Historical Purchases from Areas in the same Country
Variable Definition: vAreaPurchases represents historical actual electricity transfers into an area from other areas within the same country (province to province or state to state). This variable is an exogenous input to the model via vData.accdb.

Descriptor: Historical Sales to Areas in the same Country
Variable Definition: vAreaSales represents historical total electricity transferred out of an area to other areas within the same country (province to province or state to state). This variable is an exogenous input to the model via vData.accdb.
vCExports are the exogenously input levels of coal exports by province.

Cogeneration Generation (CgGen), the amount of generation from cogenerating facilities, depends on five factors:  the Cogeneration Normal Capacity Utilization Factor for policies (CgCUFP), the Cogeneration Utilization Factor (CgUMS), Cogeneration Capacity (CgGC), the Cogeneration Capacity Utilization Factor (CgCUF) and the Economic Capacity Utilization Factor (WCUF or ECUF).  The utilization factors perform the same functions as their counterparts that modify demand.  Cogeneration Generation (CgEC) is accounted for in two ways.  First the electricity generated is tracked in kWh.  It is also, when multiplied by the Cogeneration Heat Rate (CgHrt), tracked by a variable called Cogeneration Energy Demands (CgDmd) and is added to final net electric demand (ECD).  ECD is electric DMD minus the energy needed to cogenerate.  ECD is very close to electric sales.
vCImports are the exogenously input levels of coal imports by province.
Coal production (vCProd) is the exogenously input coal production by area historically as well as projections through 2050.

Degree days (vDDay) are measures of how cold or warm a location is. A degree day compares the mean (the average of the high and low) outdoor temperatures recorded for a location to a standard temperature. The more extreme the outside temperature, the higher the number of degree days. A high number of degree days generally results in higher levels of energy use for space heating or cooling.

DEE(Marginal Device Efficiency is the ratio of energy into the device to the useable energy out of it.  The DEE is determined by the fuel prices(ECFP).  As prices rise, marginal device efficiencies rise as well.  As with marginal process efficiency, once DEE is known, Device Capital Costs(DCC) are also known through the estimated trade-off curves.  How much additional device efficiency will be selected as prices rise depends on consumer preferences for higher upfront capital costs over higher operating costs in the future. All calculations are performed in the demand sector.

vDEETrend is an input to the model and represents the trend in device efficiency for particular end uses over time. An actual device efficiency is input to the model for only one reference year (a year in which data were available). The device efficiency default levels are then determined based on the exogenously input trend.

The DDM is one of two variables represent the effects of weather on energy demand - the other is the fraction of Temperature Sensitive Load(TSLOAD).  Each end-use has a specified fraction of its load designated as temperature-sensitive.  This fraction ranges from zero to one.  This portion of the load is adjusted in response to a change in the degree day multiplier(DDM - usually set equal to one).  If a period of time is expected to have warmer or cooler temperatures than normal, the DDM is adjusted to reflect this expected temperature increase or decrease and the energy demand from temperature sensitive load is adjusted accordingly.  This variable is generally used when developing alternative scenarios on which to test policies - for example to determine the effects of global warming.  It can also be used to model weather patterns that are expected to differ in the short term. The baseline or default value is usually set equal to one.
vDmd is a model input of historical end use energy demands by fuel type for all industries
vDmd_NoOG is a model input of historical end use energy demands by fuel type for all industries except the oil and gas industries.
vDmd_OG is a model input of historical end use energy demands by fuel type for the oil and gas industries.
Energy Conversion Factors (vECF) are conversion factors input to the model to convert various units into petajoules (PJ).
Unit names for the energy conversion factors (vECF)

Historical total electric generation by fuel type (vEGFA) exogenously input to the model. This total generation should be equal to the total generation across all individual electric generating units historically.

Price of energy in wholesale markets by fuel type in constant dollars per mmbtu. The current model version applies the same wholesale price to all areas. This price generally reflects the sale price of a fuel by producers for the relevant market. For example, the oil wholesale price will generally be the price of oil globally.

XEUDMD is historical utility fuel emand across fuels by plant.  The demand for each fuel by plant (in "natural" units such as barrels) is found in the plant data in FERC #1.  These natural units are then converted to tBtu and summed across plant.  For example, to calculate the fuel usage of a coal plant using oil to start, oil and coal demand (in barrels and tons, respectively) would be converted to tBtus and summed.
Total exports (vExports) by fuel type and nation are an input to the model.

Descriptor: Historical Purchases from Areas in a different Country
Variable Definition: vExpPurchases are historical, exogenously input electricity purchases from areas in a different country (imports).

Descriptor: Historical Sales to Areas in a different Country
Variable Definition: vExpSales are historical, exogenously input electricity sales (exports) to areas in a different country (imports).

Descriptor: Exogenous Delivered Fuel Prices before Taxes
Variable Definition: Base fuel prices (vFPBase) are exogenously input delivered fuel prices excluding taxes and emissions costs.
Input sales fuel tax is used to calibrate fuel prices and as an basis for the forecast years
Input excise fuel tax is used to calibrate fuel prices and as an basis for the forecast years
Input historical feedstocks demands are used to calibrate the feedstock process efficiency for use in the model forecast
Input gas production by area for each type of gas production process. Used for the gas production module
Total imports (vImports) by fuel type and nation are an input to the model.
Input liquid natural gas production is used as the driver for the LNGProduction demand sector
Input oil production by area for each type of oil production process. Used for the oil production module

Input value for RPP production. Used as an input for refinery calibration and starting point for forecast values. Can be used in forecast years as an exogenous production forecast depending on the value of RPP switch
Input value for RPP exports. Used to calibrate refinery segment
Input value for RPP imports. Used to calibrate refinery segment
Input fuel demands for steam generation
Input historical energy demands for the transportation segment.
Input historical demands for fuel feedstocks for the transportation segment
vCogFPol is the exogenous input historical emissions from cogeneration by industry, pollutant, and area.  This variable gets written to the model variable, XCgFPol.
vEnFPol is historical, exogensouly input total energy-related pollution by fuel type and ECC.
Electric utility pollution coefficients (vEUPOCX) are an input to the model by fuel source, plant type, pollutant, and area.

Emissions due to gas flaring (zFlPol) from leaking gas into the air. Flaring is conducted in oil refineries, oil wells, and chemical plants and is often done as a safety measure to release over-pressuring of pipes. Emissions from flaring are calculated from a flaring emissions coefficient applied to the process emissions driver minus and flaring reductions obtained and plus any venting emissions from flaring CH4.
Input value for emissions coefficients for non-combusted fuels used as raw material input for processes
Input fugitive emissions inventories is used with the historical economic driver to calculate the fugitive emissions coefficient
Input non-enegy emissions inventories. This is used with the historical economic driver to produce a non-energy emissions coefficient.
Input energy emissions for offroad vehicles for non-transportation sectors. Inventories are used with historical energy demands to produce energy emissions coefficients for the forecast
Input non-energy emissions for offroad vehicles for non-transportation sectors. Inventories are used with the economic driver to produce non-energy emissions coefficients for the forecast

Marginal emissions per fuel used for new devices is input directly for GHG pollutants and is calculated using input inventories for CAC pollutants. This value is applied to new devices added annually by the model as an input for the total stock emissions coefficient (POCA)
Input energy based emission inventories for the transportation segment. Historical inventories are compared with historical demands to produce energy-based emissions coefficients.
Input fuel feedstock emissions coefficient for the transportation segment as the amount of emissions per unit of feedstock demand.
Input non-energy emissions for the transportation segment. Input inventories are used historically to calculate the non-energy emissions coefficient based on historical levels of economic driver.
Input energy based emissions coefficients for the transporation segment. Value reflects the tonnes of pollution emitted per Tbtu of energy consumed.

Venting emissions are produced as a function of the input venting emissions coefficient, the economic driver, and any venting emissions reductions. These emissions are considered driven by economic activity, not energy consumption.
